skill.name: helpdesk-automation
skill.description: Automates HelpDesk ticket browsing, saved view inspection, canned response lookup, and custom field review through the Rube MCP integration.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP to inspect HelpDesk tickets, views, canned replies, and custom fields.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static shell-execution and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives caused by markdown inline code, frontmatter, and tool names in SKILL.md. The confirmed risk is legitimate but meaningful: the skill requires a third-party Rube MCP endpoint and HelpDesk authentication, so ticket data may pass through an external integration.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Endpoint Handles HelpDesk Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and use it for HelpDesk operations. This is expected behavior, but it creates a third-party network dependency for support ticket data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded MCP endpoint is explicitly documented and tied to HelpDesk automation. This is legitimate integration behavior, but it materially affects data exposure and trust boundaries.

medium_findings.title: HelpDesk Authentication Is Delegated Through Rube MCP
medium_findings.description: The setup flow tells users to manage a HelpDesk connection and follow an authentication link when the connection is not active. This can grant ticket-system access through the external MCP provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly require HelpDesk connection management and authentication. No malicious intent is shown, but the access delegation should be reviewed before publication.

low_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts are false positives. The referenced backticks are markdown formatting for MCP tool names, parameters, and examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected file is markdown documentation and contains no executable Ruby code or shell command invocation. The flagged backticks delimit inline code and fenced examples.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are not supported by semantic context. SKILL.md contains helpdesk workflow documentation and no cryptographic functions, algorithms, hashes, or encryption operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only prose, tool names, and parameter descriptions at the reported locations. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is Benign Rate-Limit Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert is a false positive. The relevant line advises keeping page sizes reasonable to avoid HelpDesk API timeouts, not collecting host or environment details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is operational guidance about API pagination and timeouts. No commands, probes, host inventory, or environment discovery instructions are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network Service Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on the Rube MCP endpoint for HelpDesk operations. Users should confirm data handling terms and permissions before connecting production support accounts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and integration purpose are explicitly documented. The risk is a trust-boundary concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Support teams lose time moving between ticket lists, views, templates, and field definitions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP workflows for faster HelpDesk inspection.
actual_capabilities: Lists HelpDesk tickets by silo, sort field, order, and page size.
Explains cursor-based pagination using next and previous cursor values.
Lists saved HelpDesk views that agents use to organize ticket queues.
Retrieves canned response templates for common support replies.
Lists custom field definitions configured for the HelpDesk account.
Directs the assistant to search Rube tools first for current schemas.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP connection and a HelpDesk account connection.
Current documented workflows focus on read and list operations.
Ticket creation, updates, replies, and view changes may need other tools.
Results depend on HelpDesk permissions, rate limits, and available MCP schemas.

use_cases.title: Review Open Ticket Queues
use_cases.description: Browse active, archived, trash, or spam ticket silos with predictable pagination and sorting.

use_cases.title: Audit Response Templates
use_cases.description: Retrieve canned responses so teams can review available templates before replying to customers.

use_cases.title: Map HelpDesk Data Fields
use_cases.description: Inspect custom field definitions before building reports, routing rules, or data quality checks.

prompt_templates.title: List Recent Tickets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the HelpDesk automation skill to list recent active tickets. Search Rube tools first, then show the newest tickets with a page size of 20.

prompt_templates.title: Browse a Ticket Silo
prompt_templates.prompt: List HelpDesk tickets from the archive silo, sorted by updatedAt in descending order. Include the cursor details needed for the next page.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Support Templates
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for canned response review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve the available HelpDesk canned responses. Summarize each template name, intended use, and any placeholders that need manual values.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Account Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the current Rube HelpDesk schemas, list saved views and custom fields, then identify how the account organizes ticket work.

output_examples.input: List the newest active HelpDesk tickets.
output_examples.output: A concise ticket list with ticket identifiers, current silo, creation or update time, and a note when another page is available.

output_examples.input: Show the canned responses available to agents.
output_examples.output: A grouped summary of response templates, including template purpose, placeholders, and any formatting notes found in the response content.

output_examples.input: Explain the HelpDesk custom fields for this account.
output_examples.output: A field inventory that names each custom field, describes its type, and highlights validation or configuration details.

best_practices: Search Rube tools before each workflow so parameter schemas match the current integration.
Use small page sizes when exploring tickets to reduce timeouts and rate-limit pressure.
Confirm the HelpDesk connection status is active before requesting ticket data.
anti_patterns: Do not assume ticket cursors are optional when moving between pages.
Do not mix forward and backward pagination cursors in one request.
Do not treat read-only listing workflows as ticket update or reply tools.

faq.question: Does this skill require Rube MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. It is written for the Rube MCP HelpDesk toolkit and requires that MCP server to be connected.

faq.question: Can it create or update tickets?
faq.answer: The documented workflows focus on listing and inspection. Creation and updates may require additional tools.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameter names and requirements.

faq.question: What HelpDesk data can it inspect?
faq.answer: It can inspect ticket lists, saved views, canned responses, and custom field definitions through available tools.

faq.question: Is it safe for production support data?
faq.answer: It can be used if your organization approves Rube MCP access and reviews HelpDesk permissions before connection.

faq.question: How does pagination work?
faq.answer: Ticket browsing uses timestamp and ticket ID cursor pairs for forward or backward navigation.
